entry.title: はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - 動的テキスト
entry.content:
本ブログシリーズはSAP Analytics CloudのBI機能を使ってダッシュボードやレポートの作成を行う方を対象とし、SAP Analytics CloudのBIにおける基本の操作やTipsをハンズオン形式でご紹介します。

本ブログでは、フィルタ条件や選択内容をテキストとして画面上に明示したい際に使用いただける動的テキストの作成についてご紹介いたします。

 

目次：

  • SAP Analytics Cloudにおける動的テキストの概要
  • 動的テキストの作成方法[ハンズオン]

※ベースとなるサンプルストーリーの作成⼿順は、はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - サンプルモデル／ストーリーの作成 - SAP Communityでご紹介しています。
※ SAP Analytics Cloudは、四半期に１度のペースで製品のバージョンアップが実施されます。そのため今後のバージョンアップにより、本ブログで紹介する画⾯キャプチャや、操作が異なる可能性があることをご了承ください。（本ブログは2026年2⽉に作成しています。）

 

SAP Analytics Cloudにおける動的テキストの概要

動的テキストはSACのストーリー内に表示する文章/チャート/テーブルなどのタイトルに、フィルタや入力コントロール、モデル変数などの条件を自動的に反映させる仕組みです。ユーザーが地域や期間などの条件を切り替えると、それに応じてテキストの内容も変わり、表示しているデータの文脈を明確に伝えられます。

例えば「現在〇〇地域の売上を表示しています」や「対象期間：2024年第3四半期」のように、分析対象を明示することで、グラフや数値だけでは分かりにくい条件を補足できます。

PDFとしてエクスポートした場合も選択中の条件が反映されたテキストが残るため、資料としての説明力も向上します。

詳しくは SAP Help Portal も参照ください：
チャートのタイトルに対する動的テキストの追加 | SAP Help Portal
テーブルのタイトルに対する動的テキストの追加 | SAP Help Portal
ストーリーへの動的テキストの追加 | SAP Help Portal

 

動的テキストの作成方法

0. はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - サンプルモデル／ストーリーの作成で作成したストーリーを編集モードで開こう！

※はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - フィルタの作成 - SAP Communityを事前に実施されている方は0.は不要です。作成されたストーリーを複製して1.のハンズオンを開始してください。

0.1. ナビゲーションバーのファイルより作成したストーリーを開き、「編集」をクリックします。

 

0.2. 「Page_1」の右横の「v」をクリックし、「複製」をクリックしてページを複製します。

 

0.3. 「Page_1 (1)」の右横の「v」をクリックし、「タイトルの編集」をクリックし、タイトルを「動的テキスト」に変更します。

 

0.4. 表示のメニューから「左側のパネル」をクリックし、アセットを表示させます。

　0.5. 「入力コントロール」をドラッグアンドドロップでストーリーの任意の場所に配置します。

 

0.6. 「ディメンション」をクリックし、「事業セグメント」、「メンバーによるフィルタ」をクリックします。

 

 

0.7. 「すべてのメンバー」にチェックを入れ、「OK」をクリックします。

フィルタの作成についてはこちらをご覧ください：はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - フィルタの作成 - SAP Community

 

1. 動的テキストを作成しよう！

1.1. 数値チャートのタイトルに動的テキストを適用します。「売上高」と表示されているテキストを選択し、マウスを右クリックします。

 

1.2.「＋追加」から、「動的テキスト」をクリックします。

 

 

1.3. 動的テキストの挿入のメニューが出るので、「入力コントロール」、「事業セグメント」を選択し、「作成」をクリックします。
「○○の売上高」となるように、「売上高」の前に「の」を追加すると見やすくなります。

 

1.4. 動的テキストを作成することができました！
入力コントロールでベストITSを選択すると、テキストも連動してベストITSになっていることが分かります。

 

　1.5. 左上の「保存」をクリックして、ストーリーを保存します。

 

今回は動的テキストのご紹介でした。SAP Analytics Cloudは他にも様々な表現や便利な機能があり、本ブログシリーズでご紹介しています。はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - シリーズまとめ - SAP Communityからご参照ください。

entry.title: Beyond the Workhorse: What I’ve Learned Modernizing U.S. Utilities
entry.content:
Introduction

In my decade-plus of architecting for major U.S. utilities—from the high-pressure power corridors of the Northeast to the expanding grids in the South—I’ve seen a shift in what "success" looks like. For a long time, the goal was simple: Keep the lights on. But as we hit 2026, that benchmark is failing. Aggressive decarbonization and the wave of decentralized energy (DERs) like rooftop solar and EV charging are putting a massive strain on our trusted workhorse, SAP IS-U.

1.     The Reality Check: When the "Standard" Hits the Ceiling

IS-U is a dependable system for meter-to-cash, but it was designed for a world of predictable, one-way power flow. In my recent field work, I've kept hitting three "Legacy Burdens":

  • The Batch Job Bottleneck: Waiting for overnight runs to understand grid load is a non-starter in a smart-grid world.
  • The "Spaghetti" Problem: Years of custom ABAP "Z-code" have made some environments so brittle that a simple integration takes months of testing.
  • The 2027 Clock: S/4HANA migration isn't just an upgrade; it’s a race to preserve business logic buried in 20 years of customizations.

 

2.     My Architecture Philosophy: The "Clean Core" Mindset

The biggest shift I’ve driven in recent programs is moving away from the instinct to "customize inside the ERP." By adopting a Clean Core strategy, we treat SAP BTP as our innovative layer.

This keeps the S/4HANA core stable. While the core handles the heavy transactions, the "brain" (SAP BTP) manages the fast-changing logic. This gives us speed without risking the stability of the billing engine.

3.     Practical Field Notes: Real Impact with BTP

This isn't theoretical. I’ve seen this move the needle in North American programs, including work with one of the largest utility companies in north America:

  • Integration Suite: This is the "glue" connecting AMI, weather data, and regulatory feeds without brittle, point-to-point interfaces.
  • Hindsight to Foresight: We are now using SAP Datasphere to identify transformer stress before it leads to failure.
  • The Human Element: Customer Satisfaction (CSAT) rises when we use BTP to send proactive usage alerts mid-month.

4.     A Deep Dive: The Automated Reconnection Case

I’m particularly proud of rebuilding the customer reconnection process. Traditionally, this was a multi-day headache. We turned it into a real-time event.

How we built it:

  1. Payment Trigger: Customer pays via an app (SAP Build).
  2. Instant Clearing: SAP Integration Suite pushes the update to FI-CA.
  3. Decision & Execution: We applied a 3-layered governance protocol to ensure only "Safe-to-Reconnect" signals hit the AMI smart meters.

4.     Lessons from the Trenches: What Leaders Need to Know

This isn't just about clicking buttons in the BTP cockpit; it's a cultural shift. If you are leading a team, remember:

  • Close the Skill Gap: Veteran ABAP teams have the domain knowledge—help them translate that into Node.js and CAP.
  • Avoid "App Sprawl": Without governance, you’ll end up with a hundred disconnected micro-apps.
  • Data Residency: For U.S. utilities, compliance for billing data must be built into the architecture from Day 1.

Conclusion: The Future is Composed

Across the programs I’ve led, the winning pattern is clear: S/4HANA is the Heart, BTP is the Brain, and IoT/Integration is the Senses.

SAP IS-U has served us well, but the energy transition demands more agility. The question isn't if we should move to BTP—it's how quickly we can use it to stop being reactive and start being proactive.

I'd love to hear from other architects: What's the messiest "Z-code" logic you're currently trying to move out of your core? Let's discuss in the comments.

entry.title: Story Versioning in SAP Analytics Cloud: Design with Confidence & Iterate Without Fear!
entry.content:
Designing impactful stories in SAP Analytics Cloud is rarely a straight line. Instead, Story Designers iterate constantly by experimenting with layouts, refining narratives, adjusting calculations, and incorporating stakeholder feedback. Until now, that iterative process often came with a trade-off: move fast, or play it safe.

Without a native way to manage major story iterations, designers relied on manual copies, naming conventions like “Corporate_Overview_Final_v7_REAL_FINAL,” or external documentation to track changes. This made it harder to experiment confidently, collaborate transparently, and recover from changes that didn’t land as expected.

With the 2026 Q1 QRC, we’re introducing Story Versioning. A foundational capability that gives Story Designers the freedom to iterate, experiment, and refine their work, while staying fully in control.

Story Versioning

Story Versioning is designed to be simple by default, while still supporting real-world design workflows. There’s no separate tool or workflow to learn as it is embedded directly into the existing authoring experience, allowing designers to adopt it naturally without disrupting how they already work. Let's look into a few of the core capabilities. 

Explicit Major Versions with Designer Control

Story Designers can create up to 10 versions per story, each acting as a major version. Creating a new version is an intentional action and are never created automatically in the background.

Once the maximum of ten versions is reached, designers choose which existing version to delete before creating a new one. This approach ensures flexibility, allowing designers to retain the versions that matter most rather than assuming the oldest version is no longer relevant.

Each version can also have a descriptive note, making it easy to capture context such as review milestones, experiments, or key design changes.

View, Restore, or Save Versions Safely

Any previous version can be opened in a read-only view, allowing designers to review earlier iterations without impacting the current version.

From there, designers can either save the version as a new story or restore it. When a version is restored, SAP Analytics Cloud automatically creates a backup of the current version, ensuring that all changes remain reversible and no work is lost unintentionally.

Always Aligned with the Latest Dependent Objects

Stories often consume multiple dependent objects such as models, themes, composites, and so on, each with its own lifecycle. Story Versioning always consumes the latest state of these dependent objects.

Predictable Transport Behavior

When transporting a story using the Content Network (ACN), only the current active version is included. This keeps transports clean and predictable, while aligning with existing lifecycle and promotion workflows.

Version Visibility in the File Repository

All created versions are visible through the file repository, giving designers transparency into a story’s evolution directly from content management views.

 

Why Story Versioning Matters

Story Versioning fundamentally changes how Story Designers work in SAP Analytics Cloud.

It enables confident experimentation without fear of breaking a story, clearer tracking across major design milestones, safer collaboration when incorporating feedback, and simpler recovery from changes that don’t pan out.

Most importantly, it shifts story design from a cautious, copy-heavy workflow to one that encourages creative exploration with guardrails. This capability also lays the foundation for future versioning for other objects in SAP Analytics Cloud, allowing all personas to focus less on managing risk and more on completing their tasks.

entry.title: Expanding Visual Analysis: New Area Chart, Pareto Chart, and Stacked Area Enhancements
entry.content:
As analytics adoption grows, Story Designers are increasingly expected to move beyond static reporting and deliver visual analysis that explains trends, highlights drivers, and supports prioritization.

While SAP Analytics Cloud already offers a strong foundation of visualizations, designers often faced limitations when trying to identify key contributors behind a metric, show how values evolve over time without visual clutter, or clearly communicate proportional impact and cumulative effects.

With the 2026 Q1 QRC, SAP Analytics Cloud expands its out-of-the-box visualization capabilities with new and enhanced charts: Pareto Chart, Area Chart, and improvements to the Stacked Area!

Pareto Chart: Focus on What Truly Drives Impact

The Pareto Chart is designed to help Story Designers to prioritize focus, explain trade-offs, and guide action instead of treating all contributors as equally important.

We wanted the creation of a Pareto Chart to be intentionally simple. Story Designers just have to bind a primary structure (Measure or Account depending on the Model Type) and select the dimension across which the analysis should run. The Pareto Visualization is then automatically generated, sorted from highest to lowest, including the cumulative percentage which increases to 100%. 

Furthermore, we allow additional Primary Structures as supplementary columns for context without impacting the Cumulative Percentage Calculation. It provides Story Designers the option to enrich the analysis while keeping the pareto logic clean and mathematically correct.

⚠️ For the Cumulative Percentage, Pareto Charts rely on a Running Total Calculation and therefore is not supported for data models that do not allow for the creation of a Running Total Calculation in Stories, such as SAP BW and SAP IBP. 

Area Chart: A Clear Alternative to Stacked Visuals

The new Area Chart introduces a powerful option for visualizing trends over time while maintaining focus on the overall shape and direction of the data. Compared to Stacked Area Charts, Area Charts emphasize trend and magnitude rather than composition, reduce visual complexity when multiple series are present, and make it easier to follow a single measure’s evolution across time. It makes Area Charts especially valuable when the analytical goal is trend interpretation rather than part-to-whole comparison.

Stacked Area Chart Enhancements: More Expressive, More Flexible

The Stacked Area Chart has been enhanced to support use cases that matter to you the most! These include the introduction of a 100% Stacked Area Chart, enabling designers to clearly visualize proportional contribution over time. We've also added the configuration to adjust the line to a smooth line, which also applies to the Area Chart and offers consistency with our other Chart Types. 

Why This Matters for Story Designers

These visualization enhancements significantly expand what Story Designers can do out of the box in SAP Analytics Cloud rather than relying on Custom Widgets.

As SAP Analytics Cloud continues to evolve its visualization portfolio, designers can rely less on workarounds and more on purpose-built visuals that match analytical intent.

ⓘ Keep an eye out on the roadmap explorer, as new Chart Types such as the Gauge, Funnel, and Sankey will be coming soon!

entry.title: From Spreadsheets to a Financial Digital Twin: Reinventing FP&A with SAP
entry.content:
What Exactly Is a Financial Digital Twin?

At its core, a financial digital twin is a virtual model of your financial reality that is:

  • Connected: Continuously fed by actuals and operational data.
  • Intelligent: Built on business drivers and assumptions, not just static accounts.
  • Interactive: Able to simulate scenarios and show impacts on P&L, balance sheet, and cash in near real time.

You can think of it as a flight simulator for your finance function: instead of testing strategies in the real world (with real risk), you run them through the twin and see how they behave under different conditions.

Why CFOs and FP&A Leaders Should Care

  1. From Static Budgets to Continuous Planning

Traditional FP&A runs on an annual budget and a handful of reforecasts. A digital twin enables:

  • Rolling forecasts that update when new data arrives, not just at quarter-end.
  • Near real-time visibility into how operational changes (orders, production, collections) flow into revenue, margins, and cash.

The result is a finance function that reacts to shocks (demand drops, price spikes, supply disruptions) in days, not months.

2. Better Decisions Through Scenario-Based Thinking

A financial digital twin is built for “what if” questions:

  • What if volume drops by 10% in our top three markets?
  • What if we move 15% of sales to a lower-margin channel?
  • What if FX moves against us by 5% for two quarters?

Instead of debating assumptions in PowerPoint, you run scenarios in the twin and see quantified impacts on earnings, leverage, and liquidity. This supports:

  • More confident guidance to the board and investors.
  • Better capital allocation and risk-management decision

 

3. A Shared View Across Finance and Operations

In many organizations, operations and finance look at different numbers and speak different languages. The digital twin creates a shared environment where:

  • Operations sees how their KPIs (throughput, yield, service levels) hit EBITDA and cash.
  • Finance sees the operational levers behind variances.

This alignment turns FP&A into a collaborative business partner rather than a reporting factory.

External Data ( machine info, Forex data etc)

The SAP Building Blocks of a Financial Digital Twin

If your core runs on SAP, you already have most of the building blocks. The opportunity is to deliberately assemble them into a coherent digital twin architecture.

  1. SAP S/4HANA (and Central Finance): The Single Source of Truth

The Universal Journal in S/4HANA gives you a single, granular source of financial and controlling data. This is the transactional backbone of your twin:

  • All postings end up in one place (ACDOCA) with rich dimensions (company code, profit center, segment, customer, product, etc.).
  • You can drill from consolidated views down to individual line items without leaving the system.

If you run multiple ERPs, Central Finance can pull these into a harmonized S/4HANA-based finance hub. The digital twin then represents the full group, not just one system.

2. SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC): Planning, Forecasting, and Decision Cockpit

SAC is where much of the FP&A “front end” of the twin lives:

  • Planning models aligned to S/4HANA dimensions and hierarchies.
  • Driver-based budgeting where users adjust volumes, prices, and headcount instead of typing values into every account.
  • Scenario management with versions for base, upside, downside, and stress-test cases.
  • Dashboards and stories to visualize actuals, plan, and forecast side by side.

With live connectivity to S/4HANA, SAC can show up-to-date actuals while keeping planning data in the same space, eliminating reconciliation headaches.

3. SAP PaPM: The Economic Engine

A true digital twin needs to reflect how value is actually created and consumed, not just how it is reported. SAP Profitability and Performance Management (PaPM) helps by:

  • Modeling complex cost and revenue allocation logic (cost-to-serve, shared services, transfer pricing).
  • Simulating the impact of changes in drivers (volumes, discounts, service levels) on product and customer profitability.

PaPM can feed its results back into SAC planning and S/4HANA reporting, turning your twin into a realistic economic model, not just a spreadsheet on steroids.

4. Group Reporting: Real-Time Group Perspective

For group-level FP&A, S/4HANA Group Reporting is critical:

  • It consolidates group financials close to the source, enabling faster closes and near real-time group views.
  • Integrated with SAC, it allows group-level planning and scenario modeling on top of live consolidated data.

This closes the gap between statutory reporting, management reporting, and FP&A scenarios.

5. SAP Signavio: The Process Twin

Finance outcomes are driven by processes: order-to-cash, procure-to-pay, record-to-report, and forecast-to-fulfill. SAP Signavio adds a process-intelligence layer:

  • Process mining shows how these processes really run (cycle times, rework, bottlenecks).
  • You can link these insights to financial impacts—e.g., how process delays affect DSO, inventory, and working capital.

When you overlay Signavio insights onto your financial twin, FP&A can model not just financial numbers but the process changes needed to achieve them.

 

 

 

Putting It Together: An SAP-Based Financial Digital Twin Architecture

At a high level, your architecture might look like this:

  1. Data foundation
    • S/4HANA (and Central Finance if needed) holds harmonized actuals and master data.
    • Group Reporting provides consolidated views.
  2. Modeling and simulation
    • PaPM and CO-PA model profitability and key drivers.
    • Signavio models and analyses process flows.
  3. Planning and analytics
    • SAC hosts planning models, scenarios, dashboards, and predictive analytics.
  4. User experience
    • FP&A, business controllers, and business leaders access insights mainly through SAC and Fiori apps.

Step-by-Step: How to Design Your Financial Digital Twin

This is where many CFOs ask, “Where do we start?” Here’s a pragmatic sequence you can follow.

Step 1: Start with Business Questions, Not Tools

Clarify the decisions you want the twin to support. Examples:

  • “How exposed is our EBITDA and cash to a 10–15% demand shock in our top three markets?”
  • “Which products and customers are truly profitable after cost-to-serve?”
  • “How much working capital can we release without hurting service levels?”

Rank these questions and commit to solving the top three in your first iteration. This ensures your twin delivers visible business value quickly.

Step 2: Harmonize Data and Dimensions in S/4HANA

A digital twin fails if it rests on inconsistent structures. Work with your finance and IT teams to:

  • Standardize chart of accounts, profit centers, cost centers, and key reporting dimensions.
  • Ensure master data (customers, products, materials) is aligned across entities.
  • Confirm that the Universal Journal captures enough detail for your FP&A use cases.

This may sound “plumbing-like,” but it is foundational. Better models cannot fix broken master data.

Step 3: Build Driver Models in PaPM and CO-PA

Once the data foundation is solid, model your business logic:

  • Identify key drivers: volumes, prices, discounts, service levels, productivity, FX rates, etc.
  • Use PaPM (and, where relevant, CO-PA) to define how these drivers flow into revenues, costs, and profitability.
  • Validate the models with business owners using historical data—do they reflect reality well enough?

Aim for models that are simple enough to understand but rich enough to support decision-making.

Step 4: Implement Driver-Based Planning and Scenarios in SAC

Next, bring the twin to life for planners and business partners:

  • Build SAC planning models aligned to S/4HANA structures (no “shadow hierarchies” in Excel).
  • Replace line-item data entry with driver-based input where possible (e.g., volume and price instead of revenue by account; FTE and merit increases instead of salary lines).
  • Configure scenario and version management so FP&A can maintain base, upside, downside, and stress scenarios in parallel.

This is where business users start to “feel” the twin: they change a driver and immediately see financial impacts.

Step 5: Connect Group-Level View with Group Reporting

If you operate as a group:

  • Integrate Group Reporting with SAC so consolidated actuals flow directly into planning models.
  • Use the same structures for management and statutory views where possible to reduce reconciliation.
  • Model group scenarios: acquisitions, divestitures, segment realignments, and their impacts on leverage and covenants.

This helps your board-level discussions move from static decks to interactive, scenario-driven conversations.

Step 6: Add Process Intelligence with SAP Signavio

Once the financial twin is running, enrich it with process insights:

  • Use Signavio to analyze critical processes affecting revenue recognition, cost, and working capital (e.g., billing, collections, procurement, production).
  • Quantify how process changes (faster collections, fewer disputes, better forecast accuracy) move financial metrics.
  • Incorporate these into your PaPM and SAC models as additional levers.

Now your twin does not just answer “What if revenue drops?” but also “What if we improve our collection process by 5 days?”

Step 7: Build a Continuous Learning Loop

A digital twin should get smarter over time:

  • In SAC, set up dashboards comparing actuals vs. plan/forecast at multiple levels (entity, product, customer, segment).
  • Track forecast accuracy and bias by business area and driver.
  • Periodically recalibrate drivers and assumptions in PaPM and SAC models based on observed performance.

Over time, this turns the twin into a trusted decision-support system rather than a one-off project.

 

 

Bringing the Twin to Life: Three FP&A Use Cases

Concrete stories make the concept real for your stakeholders. Here are three you can adapt for your industry.

Use Case 1: Protecting Margins in a Demand Downturn

A global manufacturer sees early signals of softening demand:

  • S/4HANA and SAC show a drop in order intake for specific regions and products.
  • FP&A uses the twin to model a 15% volume decline and its impact on gross margin, plant utilization, and cash over the next two quarters.
  • They test combinations of levers: pricing actions, marketing cuts, production scale-backs, and working-capital measures (inventory reductions, payment-term changes).
  • Management selects a balanced response that protects strategic investments while preserving liquidity, all backed by quantified scenarios.

Instead of reactive across-the-board cuts, the digital twin enables targeted, data-driven action.

Use Case 2: Capacity Expansion and Capex Decisions

A business unit proposes a new production line:

  • PaPM models show how higher capacity and utilization would impact fixed-cost absorption and margins.
  • Group Reporting and SAC project the effect on group-level earnings, leverage, and covenants.
  • Signavio reveals that existing logistics and order-fulfillment processes are already a bottleneck; without fixing them, the new capacity would not be fully utilized.
  • The final decision bundles capex with process improvements, all tested in the twin before committing funds.

This elevates capex approvals from “business case PDFs” to interactive, scenario-based decisions.

Use Case 3: Continuous Working-Capital Steering

Working capital is often a major, but under-optimized, source of cash:

  • The twin brings together DSO, DPO, inventory days, and order patterns from S/4HANA.
  • SAC running predictive models and driver-based forecasts shows future cash conversion under different collection, payment, and inventory strategies.
  • Treasury and FP&A jointly manage liquidity by simulating changes to terms, discount programs, and inventory policies.

Here, the digital twin becomes a daily steering tool, not just a quarterly working-capital report.

How to Talk About This Within Your Organization

For CFOs and FP&A leaders, positioning is key. A few suggestions:

  • With the board:  Emphasize resilience, speed of response, and better capital allocation, not system features.
  • With business leaders: Focus on usable scenarios, transparency on drivers, and the ability to test decisions safely before executing.
  • With IT and SAP teams: Frame it as an architecture and data challenge tied to specific business outcomes, not just another reporting project.

You are not “installing a tool”; you are building a new way for your organization to see itself and make decisions.

Final Thought: FP&A as the Architect of the Financial Digital Twin

The digital twin is a strategic opportunity for FP&A to step up as the architect of performance, not just the reporter of results. SAP gives you the building blocks, but only finance can define the questions, drivers, and scenarios that matter.

If you’re starting this journey, pick one or two high-impact use cases, assemble the right SAP components around them, and prove the value quickly. From there, your financial digital twin can expand step by step—until it becomes the default way your company plans, decides, and acts.
